title: HTTP Security Headers: Which Sites Use HSTS, CSP &
description: Original research: We analyzed HTTP security headers across 85 top websites. Only 13% score an A. Amazon and Microsoft have zero headers.

schemas.headline: HTTP Security Headers: HSTS, CSP & X-Frame-Options Adoption Across 85 Top Websites (2026)
schemas.description: Original research analyzing HTTP security header adoption rates across 85 top websites

mainEntity.name: What percentage of websites use HSTS (Strict-Transport-Security)?

acceptedAnswer.text: Based on our analysis of 85 top websites in March 2026, 82% implement the Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header, making it the most widely adopted security header.

mainEntity.name: What percentage of websites use Content-Security-Policy (CSP)?

acceptedAnswer.text: Only 52% of top websites implement Content-Security-Policy. Despite being the most powerful defense against XSS attacks, nearly half of major sites don't use it.
No. As of March 2026, amazon.com implements zero standard HTTP security headers, receiving a Grade F in our study. This is notable given Amazon processes hundreds of billions in annual transactions.

mainEntity.name: What are the most important HTTP security headers?

acceptedAnswer.text: The four most critical security headers are: Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) to force HTTPS, Content-Security-Policy (CSP) to prevent XSS, X-Content-Type-Options to block MIME sniffing, and X-Frame-Options to prevent clickjacking. Our study found adoption rates of 82%, 52%, 66%, and 69% respectively.

mainEntity.name: Which websites have the best security headers?

acceptedAnswer.text: In our study, Facebook, Instagram, and Google Cloud scored highest with 8 out of 10 security headers (Grade A). YouTube, Twitter, and Dropbox followed with 7 out of 10.
